key_figures: James the Just; Peter; John; Philip the Evangelist
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Jerusalem is founding locus; James the Just leads as first bishop; Acts 2-12 attest continuous community from Pentecost. Uncertainty: dates of individual apostolic departures vary. Evidence: Acts 2-12; Gal 1:18-19. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/2.htm ; https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

key_figures: James the Just; Peter; Paul; Barnabas
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Apostolic Council (~49 AD; Acts 15) under James decides Gentile admission; Paul's second Jerusalem visit confirmed by Gal 2. Uncertainty: council date debated 48-50 AD. Evidence: Acts 15; Gal 2:1-10. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/15.htm ; https://biblehub.com/galatians/2-1.htm

key_figures: James the Just; Simeon bar Cleophas; Paul
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Paul's final Jerusalem visit (~58 AD) and James' martyrdom (62 AD) mark transition; Simeon bar Cleophas succeeds as bishop. Evidence: Acts 21-24; Josephus, Ant. 20.9.1. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/21.htm

Summary: Roman destruction of Jerusalem (70 AD) scatters community; church reportedly fled to Pella before siege; Simeon bar Cleophas leads in exile/return. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.5; 3.11. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Reconstituted community under Simeon bar Cleophas post-70; Jewish-Christian character continues. Uncertainty: community size and exact composition after 70 unclear. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.11-3.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Simeon bar Cleophas continues as bishop; Eusebius records the Jerusalem episcopal succession list for this era. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

key_figures: Simeon bar Cleophas; Justus I; Zacchaeus
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Simeon bar Cleophas martyred c.107 by governor Atticus; succession of Jewish-Christian bishops continues. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.32; 4.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Succession of Jewish-Christian bishops at Jerusalem continues; Eusebius records the full episcopal list. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Jewish-Christian episcopate continues approaching Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135). Uncertainty: exact bishop sequence debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135) leads Hadrian to raze Jerusalem, found Aelia Capitolina, and install first Gentile bishops; Jews including Jewish Christians barred from the city. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.6; 4.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Gentile bishops continue at Aelia Capitolina; church now Gentile in composition; community rebuilds under Roman colony status. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Gentile church at Aelia Capitolina develops; Justin Martyr references Jerusalem in his apologetic writings from Rome. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Narcissus of Jerusalem emerges as celebrated bishop famous for Easter vigil miracle (water to oil). Uncertainty: dates of his episcopate vary slightly. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.12; 6.9. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Narcissus leads; Jerusalem involved in Quartodeciman Easter controversy alongside other Eastern sees. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.12; 5.23-5.25. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm
Summary: Narcissus faces controversy causing temporary retirement; subsequently restored as bishop. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.9-6.11. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

key_figures: Narcissus of Jerusalem; Alexander of Jerusalem
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Alexander of Jerusalem joins the very elderly Narcissus as co-bishop c.212 (overlap decade). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.10-6.11. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Origen visits Jerusalem c.215-216; Alexander and Narcissus invite him to preach, causing controversy with Demetrius of Alexandria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.11; 6.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Alexander leads; Origen preaches at Jerusalem and Caesarea by invitation of local bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Alexander continues as bishop; involved in Palestinian synods active in this period. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19; 6.23. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Alexander maintains close relations with Origen now settled permanently in Caesarea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.27-6.30. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Alexander dies in prison during Decian persecution (~250); Mazabanes then Hymenaeus succeed. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Decian persecution (249-251) kills Alexander; Mazabanes then Hymenaeus bishop; community recovers. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39; 7.14. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Recovery after Valerian persecution (257-260); Gallienus edict of toleration (260) provides relief for Palestinian churches. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.13. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm
Summary: Hymenaeus of Jerusalem attends synods concerning Paul of Samosata controversy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.28-7.30. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm
Summary: The Great Peace before Diocletian allows church growth; Zabdas then Hermon serve as bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Hermon then Macarius I bishop; Macarius is the last bishop before Diocletian's Great Persecution (303). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Diocletian's Great Persecution (303-312) strikes Palestine severely; Eusebius witnesses and records Palestinian martyrdoms firsthand. Evidence: Eusebius, De Martyribus Palaestinae. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250108.htm

Summary: Macarius I serves as persecution ends; Edict of Milan (313) brings religious freedom; church begins public recovery. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250110.htm

Summary: Macarius of Jerusalem attends Nicaea (325) and secures imperial support for construction of Church of Holy Sepulchre (~326). Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.25-3.40. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

Summary: Helena's pilgrimage (~326-328) and dedication of Church of Holy Sepulchre (335) transform Jerusalem into a major pilgrimage center. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.25-3.43. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre

key_figures: Maximus of Jerusalem; Cyril of Jerusalem
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Cyril of Jerusalem begins catechetical lectures; Jerusalem grows as pilgrimage destination and theological center. Evidence: Cyril of Jerusalem, Catechetical Lectures. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/3101.htm

Summary: Cyril of Jerusalem (bishop ~350) codifies catechetical practice; city firmly established as a major Christian see. Evidence: Cyril, Catechetical Lectures; Procatechesis. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/3101.htm

Summary: Disciples first called 'Christians' at Antioch (Acts 11:26); Barnabas arrives from Jerusalem, then summons Paul from Tarsus. Uncertainty: tradition of Petrine co-founding is strong but secondary. Evidence: Acts 11:19-26. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/11-26.htm

Summary: Antioch base for Paul's 1st and 2nd missionary journeys; Peter visits causing the Antioch incident (Gal 2:11-14). Evidence: Acts 13-15; Gal 2:11-14. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13.htm ; https://biblehub.com/galatians/2-11.htm
Summary: Paul departs Antioch for 3rd missionary journey (Acts 18:22-23); Antioch is established sending community. Evidence: Acts 18:22-23. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/18-22.htm
Summary: Evodius serves as first named post-apostolic bishop; Ignatius of Antioch follows (~c.69-107). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.22. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Ignatius of Antioch leads; his letters presuppose a monarchical bishop with presbyters and deacons at multiple named cities. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0109.htm

Summary: Ignatius bishops Antioch; his seven letters address Ephesus, Magnesia, Tralles, Rome, Philadelphia, Smyrna, and Polycarp. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0109.htm

Summary: Ignatius arrested and transported to Rome for martyrdom (~107-117); his letter corpus is primary evidence for early episcopal order. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.36. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0109.htm

Summary: Ignatius of Antioch arrested and transported to Rome for martyrdom (~107-117); Heron succeeds as bishop; letters widely circulate. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.22; 3.36. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0109.htm
Summary: Post-Ignatian episcopal succession continues; Antiochene church maintains structured life. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.20. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Succession continues toward Theophilus of Antioch; the church contends with Marcionite and gnostic influences. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.20; 4.24. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Theophilus of Antioch writes apologetic works (Ad Autolycum) defending monotheism and Scripture; combats Marcionite influence. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.24. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0204.htm

Summary: Theophilus writes Ad Autolycum (c.175-180), the first use of the word 'Trinity' in Christian literature. Evidence: Theophilus, Ad Autolycum 2.15. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0204.htm

Summary: Antioch is leading Syrian theological center; Maximin leads; Montanist controversy debated in Syrian synods c.160s-170s. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Antioch stable under Maximin; Serapion becomes bishop c.190 and writes against Marcionites and rejects Gospel of Peter. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.19; 6.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Serapion of Antioch (c.190-211) bishop; writes on Easter controversy and rejects Gospel of Peter from use at Rhossus. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22; 6.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Serapion continues as bishop; involved in Easter controversy letters; rejects Gospel of Peter circulating at Rhossus. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Serapion leads until ~211; Asclepiades succeeds; Antioch is a major Syrian see with well-established episcopal structure. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Asclepiades succeeds Serapion (~211); Antioch remains major Eastern see during Severan era. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.12; 6.21. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Philetus then Zebinus bishop; connections with Origen during his prolific Caesarean period. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.21. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Babylas of Antioch (bishop c.237-253) reportedly denied Emperor Philip the Arab entrance without penance; martyred under Decius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29; 6.39. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Babylas of Antioch martyred under Decius (~250); his relics later transferred to Daphne by Gallus Caesar, provoking conflict. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Fabius of Antioch sympathetic to lapsed Christians in post-Decian controversy; Demetrianus succeeds ~252. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39; 6.46. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Paul of Samosata becomes bishop (~260); his dynamic monarchianism provokes multiple synods condemning him (268). Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.27-7.30. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Paul of Samosata formally deposed by synod (268); Domnus succeeds; controversy shaped later Christological language. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Domnus leads post-Paul of Samosata recovery; Antioch restored to proto-orthodox network; relative peace. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Antioch under stable succession through late 3rd century; church survived major theological crises. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Lucian of Antioch martyred 312 during Diocletian persecution; his biblical scholarship and theology influenced Arius and the Arian movement. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 9.6. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250109.htm

Summary: Philogonius bishop at Edict of Milan (313); Eustathius of Antioch (c.324-330) emerges as staunch Nicene defender. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250110.htm

Summary: Eustathius attends Nicaea (325) as leading Nicene voice; deposed by Arian-sympathetic council (~330). Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.7. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

Summary: Eustathius deposed by Arian-sympathetic council (~330); Antioch divided between Nicene and Arian factions. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.21. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Eustathius-of-Antioch
Summary: Antioch under Arian influence; Nicene minority persists; Meletian schism developing. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Leontius bishop under Arian influence; Nicene Meletian schism developing; Antioch key site of Arian controversy under Constantius II. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.28. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Paul's conversion on road to Damascus (~33-35 AD) and baptism by Ananias; a Christian community pre-existed Paul's arrival. Evidence: Acts 9:1-22; Gal 1:17. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/9.htm ; https://biblehub.com/galatians/1-17.htm

Summary: Paul escapes Damascus in a basket (Acts 9:23-25; 2 Cor 11:32-33); community continues under local leadership. Evidence: Acts 9:23-25; 2 Cor 11:32-33. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/9-23.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues as established community within the broader Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source documents Damascus specifically in this decade. Evidence: Implied in broader Syrian records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Damascus church persists post-70 destruction of Jerusalem; likely absorbs some refugees from Palestinian Christian community. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues; Ignatius' letters imply a well-networked Syrian Christian community though they do not address Damascus directly. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles (implied Syrian network). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0109.htm

Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian network. Uncertainty: specific Damascus records absent for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Damascus church part of the broader Syrian episcopal network under Antiochene influence. Uncertainty: no independent primary source. Evidence: Antiochene records; general Syrian church tradition. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues as part of Syrian ecclesiastical network; Ignatius' letter corpus presupposes well-structured Syrian Christianity. Evidence: Ignatius, Epistles (implied). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0109.htm
Summary: Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Uncertainty: specific records absent. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues; no independent primary documentation. Uncertainty: specific records absent. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian network; Justin Martyr and contemporaries attest broad Syrian Christianity but not Damascus specifically. Evidence: Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0128.htm
Summary: Damascus church probable in Syrian episcopal network. Uncertainty: no specific documentation. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian episcopal network; Theophilus of Antioch attests broad Syrian Christianity. Evidence: Theophilus, Ad Autolycum. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0204.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues in Syrian network; Serapion of Antioch presupposes well-networked Syrian Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Damascus church part of Antiochene Syrian network; Origen visits Syria broadly in this era. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Evidence: General Syrian records; Antiochene tradition. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues in Syrian network. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Syrian church records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Damascus church continues; Paul of Samosata controversy at Antioch (260s) will later affect broader Syrian church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.29. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Damascus church probable; Decian persecution (249-251) affects the broader Syrian and Palestinian church network. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Damascus church probable; Decian persecution disrupts Syrian Christianity broadly. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Damascus church probable; recovery after Valerian persecution (257-260); Gallienus edict of toleration. Evidence: General Syrian records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Damascus church probable within Syrian network affected by Paul of Samosata controversy at Antioch. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.30. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm
Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian network; Great Peace before Diocletian allows church growth. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm
Summary: Damascus church probable; pre-Diocletian peace allows development. Evidence: General Syrian records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Damascus church probable; Diocletian's Great Persecution (303-312) affects the entire Eastern church network including Syria. Evidence: Eusebius, De Martyribus Palaestinae. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250108.htm

Summary: Damascus church attested within Syrian episcopal network; Edict of Milan (313) brings freedom; Syrian churches publicly emerge. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250110.htm

Summary: Damascus participates in the broader Syrian ecclesiastical post-Nicaea context as part of Antiochene network. Evidence: Council records via Eusebius. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325
Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian Nicene network; affected by Arian controversy developing at Antioch. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm
Summary: Damascus church part of Syrian network affected by Arian dominance at Antioch. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Damascus church an established see in post-Nicaea period; part of contested Syrian ecclesiastical landscape. Evidence: Sozomen; Theodoret. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Philip the Evangelist settles in Caesarea (Acts 21:8); Peter's baptism of Cornelius here (~40 AD) is a pivotal Gentile admission event. Evidence: Acts 10; Acts 21:8. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/10.htm ; https://biblehub.com/acts/21-8.htm

Summary: Philip remains in Caesarea (Acts 21:8 shows daughters prophesying decades later); Paul passes through on journeys (Acts 9:30; 18:22). Evidence: Acts 9:30; 18:22. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/21-8.htm
Summary: Paul imprisoned at Caesarea Maritima for ~2 years (~58-60 AD); Philip the Evangelist's four daughters prophesy here. Evidence: Acts 21-27. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/23.htm

Summary: Caesarea Maritima is the Roman provincial capital; Christian community continues; Philip likely resident. Uncertainty: no specific documentation post-Acts for this decade. Evidence: Acts 21:8 (implied continued presence). Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/21-8.htm

Summary: Caesarea Maritima is developing episcopal see; the Roman administrative importance of the city makes it a natural center. Uncertainty: no independent primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Palestinian records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Caesarea Maritima continues as important see in Roman Palestine. Uncertainty: no specific primary source for this decade. Evidence: General Palestinian records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Caesarea has an established episcopal see referenced in 2nd-century records; important as Roman administrative capital of Judaea. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.5 (references earlier Caesarean bishops). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm
Summary: Caesarean church continues in the Roman administrative capital. Evidence: Eusebius references earlier Caesarean bishops. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Caesarean church continues; approaching Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135) which reshapes Palestinian Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Post-Bar Kokhba (135) Caesarea becomes the provincial capital of Syria Palaestina; the church here grows in significance. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.12. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Caesarea Maritima is the Roman provincial capital of Syria Palaestina; Christian community develops in this important context. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm
Summary: Caesarean church continuing; Justin Martyr's connections to Palestine include Caesarea-area traditions. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Caesarean church growing in the provincial capital; the see will become important under Origen and Eusebius. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Caesarean church continues as part of Palestinian episcopal network. Evidence: Eusebius references Palestinian bishops of this era generally. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Caesarean church continuing; Irenaeus' contacts in Palestine imply an active episcopal network here. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.22. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Theoctistus of Caesarea is the known bishop of this era; he will later invite Origen to preach, provoking controversy with Demetrius. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

key_figures: Theoctistus of Caesarea; Alexander of Jerusalem; Origen (visiting)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Theoctistus and Alexander of Jerusalem invite Origen to preach at Caesarea (c.215-216), causing conflict with Demetrius of Alexandria. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Theoctistus leads; Origen invited to preach at Caesarea by local bishops. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Theoctistus continues as bishop; the Caesarean church plays a key role in Palestinian synods. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Origen settles permanently in Caesarea (~232) after expulsion from Alexandria; founds Christian School of Caesarea; prolific scholarship here. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.23-6.27. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Origen

Summary: Origen produces Hexapla and massive commentary corpus at Caesarea; Pamphilus later builds library here preserving Origen's works. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.24; 6.32. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Origen

Summary: Origen tortured under Decian persecution (~250) and dies (~254); Caesarea School continues; Pamphilus preserves Origen's legacy. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.39; 6.43. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Origen

Summary: Pamphilus of Caesarea builds library and transmits Origen's legacy; he will write an Apology for Origen. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.5. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Pamphilus active at Caesarea; Theotecnus bishop; Caesarea is a major center of biblical scholarship. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm
Summary: Pamphilus oversees Caesarean library; Eusebius of Caesarea studies under him here. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.32. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

key_figures: Pamphilus of Caesarea; Eusebius of Caesarea

key_figures: Pamphilus of Caesarea; Eusebius of Caesarea

evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Athanasius exiled multiple times by Arian-sympathetic emperors; Julius I of Rome supports him; the 'Athanasius contra mundum' period. Evidence: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2811.htm

modern_denom_mapping: Coptic Orthodox; Greek Orthodox of Alexandria; Catholic

Summary: Paul arrives in Ephesus on 3rd journey (~53-54 AD); Aquila and Priscilla disciple Apollos; Paul spends over 2 years. Evidence: Acts 18:18-28; Acts 19. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/19.htm

Summary: Paul spends 2+ years in Ephesus; silversmith riot (Acts 19:23-41); Timothy serves as resident leader; Paul writes 1 Corinthians from Ephesus. Evidence: Acts 19; 1 Cor 16:8. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/19-23.htm

key_figures: Timothy; John the Apostle (trad.); unnamed bishops
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Timothy serves as leader (1 Timothy 1:3); John the Apostle traditionally moves to Ephesus post-70 with Mary; Ephesus becomes central to Asian Christianity. Evidence: 1 Timothy 1:3; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.1. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0501.htm

Summary: John the Apostle traditionally resident in Ephesus in old age; Revelation addressed to the Seven Asian Churches (Rev 2:1-7 to Ephesus) from Patmos. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.23; Revelation 2:1-7. Citations: https://biblehub.com/revelation/2-1.htm

Summary: John the Apostle traditionally dies at Ephesus in old age during Trajan's reign (~c.100 AD); his Gospel and letters are linked to this period and location. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.23. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Ignatius of Antioch writes to the Ephesians on his way to martyrdom; the Ephesian church is praised for orthodoxy and hospitality. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Ephesians. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0104.htm

Summary: Ignatius' letter to Ephesus names their bishop Onesimus (possibly the Onesimus of Philemon?); the church is active and well-organized. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Ephesians 1. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0104.htm

Summary: Revelation 2:8-11 addresses the church at Smyrna; Polycarp is associated with Smyrna from a very early age. The community is established and facing persecution. Evidence: Revelation 2:8-11. Citations: https://biblehub.com/revelation/2-8.htm

Summary: Polycarp of Smyrna leading; Ignatius of Antioch writes to Smyrna and to Polycarp personally on his way to martyrdom. Evidence: Ignatius, To the Smyrnaeans; To Polycarp. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0108.htm

Summary: Athenian church continues; Dionysius the Areopagite is traditionally the first bishop of Athens. Uncertainty: the tradition is late and the identification is debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.4; 4.23. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Quadratus presents his Apology to Hadrian in Athens (~124 AD); the Athenian church is attested with an active apologetic mission. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Athenian church continues; Aristides' Apology circulates as a key early apologetic text. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.3. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1008.htm

Summary: Tradition holds Addai/Thaddaeus brought Christianity to Edessa from Jerusalem via the Abgar correspondence; scholarly consensus places first attestation much later. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: Tradition holds Aggai succeeds Addai as bishop of Edessa; no contemporary documentation. Uncertainty: the tradition is entirely post-apostolic in attestation. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm
Summary: Tradition continues through the Addai-Aggai succession; no contemporary documentation exists. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: Tradition records Palut as bishop of Edessa; scholarly opinion places his episcopate no earlier than c.200. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: Edessan tradition records Palut; the earliest plausible attestation of Christianity in Osroene comes from the late 2nd century. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: No contemporary documentation of Christianity in Edessa in this decade. Uncertainty: All sources are retrospective. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm
Summary: No contemporary documentation. Uncertainty: tradition entirely retrospective. Evidence: Doctrine of Addai. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: Bardaisan of Edessa (154-222) is born; he will become an important Syriac Christian philosopher. The king of Edessa's conversion (trad.) suggests Christian presence, though dating is debated. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Bardaisan of Edessa (154-222) grows up; Christianity is probably present in Edessa by mid-2nd century given the later attestation. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Bardaisan of Edessa (154-222) is active; he is a court theologian under King Abgar VIII; writes many Syriac hymns and theological works. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Christianity in Edessa attested by c.170-200 AD at the latest; Bardaisan is the most prominent early Christian figure. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30; Julius Africanus. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Christianity in Edessa is securely attested by c.170 AD; Bardaisan (154-222) and Palut are leading figures; the royal court of Abgar VIII is Christianized. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30; Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Bardaisan of Edessa is a major Syriac Christian philosopher; he writes The Book of the Laws of Countries; Julius Africanus visits Edessa. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Edessa is an important center of Syriac Christianity; Palut is bishop; Bardaisan writes extensively. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Bardaisan continues active until his death (222); he writes on fate, free will, and astrology from a Christian perspective. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Bardaisan (d.222) and Palut lead the Edessan Christian community; Edessa is a major center of Syriac Christianity. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes
Summary: Bardaisan dies 222; Edessa is firmly established as a center of Syriac Christian learning. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 4.30. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Bardesanes

Summary: Edessa continues as a Syriac Christian center; approaching the period when Edessa will become the heart of the Syriac theological tradition. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa
Summary: Edessa continues as important Syriac Christian center. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa
Summary: Edessa continues; Decian persecution broadly affects the wider church. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa
Summary: Edessa continues; the city passes between Roman and Sassanid control periodically. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa
Summary: Edessa continues; part of broader Syrian ecclesiastical network. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa

Summary: Edessa continues as Syriac Christian center; approaching the period of the School of Edessa's greatest influence. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa
Summary: Edessa continues as Syriac Christian center. Evidence: Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronicle_of_Edessa

Summary: Edessa continues; Diocletian persecution broadly affects Eastern Christianity; Eusebius is copying the Abgar documents around this time. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Chronicle of Edessa. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: Eusebius records the Abgar correspondence in his Ecclesiastical History (c.313), attesting Edessan Christianity's strong identity and tradition. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Summary: Edessa participates in Nicaea as part of the Eastern church; the Syriac church is developing its own theological tradition. Evidence: Nicaea records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

Summary: Ephrem the Syrian (c.306-373) is growing up in Nisibis; he will become the greatest Syriac theologian; the Syriac church is a thriving center. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Ephrem-the-Syrian

key_figures: unnamed bishops; Ephrem the Syrian (in Nisibis/later Edessa)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Edessa is an important Syriac Christian center; Ephrem the Syrian is active in Nisibis; he will come to Edessa after 363. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Ephrem-the-Syrian

Summary: Edessa is a major Syriac Christian center; the School of Edessa is developing; Ephrem the Syrian is the foremost Syriac theologian of the age. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Ephrem-the-Syrian

Summary: North African Christianity is traditionally dated earlier than the Scillitan Martyrs trial (180 AD); a Christian community at Carthage is probable by the early 2nd century. Uncertainty: earliest specific evidence dates to 180 AD. Evidence: Acts of the Scillitan Martyrs (180). Citations: https://sourcebooks.web.fordham.edu/source/scillitan-mart.asp

Summary: North African Christian community probable before the 180 AD attestation; no primary source documents Carthage in this decade specifically. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://sourcebooks.web.fordham.edu/source/scillitan-mart.asp
Summary: North African Christian community growing; probably active in Carthage by this period. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://sourcebooks.web.fordham.edu/source/scillitan-mart.asp

Summary: North African Christian community probable; Tertullian is born in Carthage c.160 AD and knows of long-established Christianity. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Tertullian (c.160-225) is born in Carthage; his writings assume a large established Christian community with its own traditions. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm
Summary: Tertullian grows up in Carthage; the North African Christian community is well-established by this point. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Tertullian is growing as a rhetorician; converts to Christianity ~c.193-197; the Carthaginian church is substantial. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Scillitan Martyrs trial held AT Carthage (180 AD) is the earliest documentary evidence of Christianity in North Africa; Tertullian converts ~c.193. Evidence: Acts of Scillitan Martyrs (180). Citations: https://sourcebooks.web.fordham.edu/source/scillitan-mart.asp

Summary: Tertullian converts (~c.193) and begins prolific writing; the Carthaginian church is large enough to be known to Rome. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.20. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Tertullian's Apologeticus (197 AD) is a major defense of Christianity addressed to Roman governors; he writes extensively defending Christianity. Evidence: Tertullian, Apology. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Perpetua and Felicitas martyred at Carthage (203 AD) under Septimius Severus; The Passion of Perpetua and Felicitas is a vivid primary source; Tertullian moving toward Montanism. Evidence: Passion of Perpetua and Felicitas. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0324.htm

Summary: Tertullian fully joins the Montanist movement (~c.207-213); writes prolifically from a rigorist perspective; Agrippinus is the earliest named bishop of Carthage. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.20. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Tertullian dies c.225; Agrippinus is the earliest attested bishop of Carthage; the North African church is the most vibrant Latin-speaking community. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.20. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: North African church active; Agrippinus convenes synods on baptism of heretics; growing theological independence of North African church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 7.3. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Cyprian of Carthage becomes bishop (~248); a wealthy Roman citizen converted c.246; he will face the Decian persecution and the question of the lapsed. Evidence: Cyprian, Epistles; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 6.43. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/5001.htm

Summary: Decian persecution (249-251) forces Cyprian to flee; he writes extensively on the lapsed; controversy with Rome over treatment of apostates. Evidence: Cyprian, De Lapsis; On the Unity of the Church. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/5001.htm

Summary: Cyprian martyred under Valerian (258 AD); he is one of the most important early church fathers and martyrs; the Valerian persecution devastates North African leadership. Evidence: Pontius, Life of Cyprian; Acta Cypriani. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/5001.htm
Summary: Carthaginian church recovers under Gallienus' peace (260); Cyprian's writings remain enormously influential. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/5001.htm
Summary: Carthaginian church growing during Great Peace; Mensurius is bishop preceding Caecilian. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Mensurius bishop; Caecilian is deacon; the Donatist controversy is brewing over the handling of scriptures during the Diocletian persecution (traditores). Evidence: Augustine, Against the Donatists. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1401.htm

Summary: Diocletian persecution (303-305) causes the traditores controversy; Mensurius is accused of handing over scriptures; seeds of Donatist schism planted. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8; Augustine. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250108.htm

Summary: Donatist schism erupts in Carthage (311-312) when Donatus contests Caecilian's ordination by Felix of Aptunga (a traditor); Council of Arles (314) upholds Caecilian. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/Council-of-Arles

Summary: North African church deeply divided between Donatists and Catholics; Nicaea (325) is attended by both sides. Evidence: Optatus of Milevis, De Schismate Donatistarum. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1710.htm

Summary: Donatist movement dominates much of North African Christianity; the schism continues despite imperial attempts at reunion. Evidence: Optatus, De Schismate. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1710.htm
Summary: Donatists dominate North African Christianity; Council of Carthage (345/348) attempts reconciliation. Evidence: Optatus, De Schismate. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1710.htm

Summary: Donatus exiled c.347 by Emperor Constans; the Donatist schism remains North Africa's central ecclesiastical crisis; Augustine will later combat it. Evidence: Optatus, De Schismate. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1710.htm

Summary: The Christian community at Lyon was founded by missionaries from Asia Minor (Greek speakers); the earliest plausible estimate for Christian presence is mid-2nd century. Uncertainty: no primary source documents Lyon before the 177 martyrdoms. Evidence: Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne (177). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Pothinus of Lyon (a Greek-speaker) arrives as bishop; the community is primarily Asian Greek in origin. Uncertainty: dates of Pothinus' arrival debated. Evidence: Letter of the Churches of Lyon and Vienne (177). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Irenaeus arrives in Lyon from Asia Minor as presbyter; Pothinus is aging bishop; the community is growing. Evidence: Irenaeus, Adv. Haer. (autobiographical sections). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0103.htm

Summary: The Martyrdoms of Lyon and Vienne (177 AD) under Marcus Aurelius; Pothinus (bishop, 90+ years old), Blandina (slave), Attalus and others martyred; Irenaeus carries letter to Rome. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1-5.3. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Irenaeus of Lyon becomes bishop (~177); writes Against Heresies (Adversus Haereses, c.177-185) - the most comprehensive anti-gnostic work of the early church. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.4; 5.20. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0103.htm

Summary: Irenaeus continues as bishop; writes Demonstration of the Apostolic Preaching; mediates between Rome and Asian churches on Easter dating. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0103.htm

key_figures: Irenaeus of Lyon (d. c.202); unnamed successor
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Irenaeus dies c.202; successors lead Lyon; the Lyonese church remains a significant Gallican center. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.24. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0103.htm
Summary: Lyon church continues as important Gallican see. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm
Summary: Lyon church continues. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Lyon church continues; Decian persecution broadly affects Gallican churches. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Lyon church continues. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm
Summary: Lyon church continues; Diocletian persecution affects Gallican churches. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250108.htm
Summary: Lyon church attested; Bishop of Lyon attends Council of Arles (314). Evidence: Council of Arles records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/Council-of-Arles
Summary: Lyon bishop attends Nicaea (325); important Western see. Evidence: Nicaea records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325
Summary: Lyon church attested in post-Nicaea network. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm
Summary: Lyon church attested. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm
Summary: Lyon is an established Western see with the legacy of Irenaeus and the 177 martyrdoms. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

key_figures: Pliny the Younger (governor, attests Bithynian Christians)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Pliny the Younger's letter to Trajan (c.111-112 AD) attests that Christians are numerous throughout Bithynia-Pontus province; Nicomedia is the provincial capital. Uncertainty: letter does not name Nicomedia specifically. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97. Citations: https://faculty.georgetown.edu/jod/texts/pliny.html

Summary: Pliny's letter to Trajan (c.111-112 AD) is the premier extrabiblical attestation of Christianity; Christians in Bithynia had abandoned their faith in the face of persecution. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97. Citations: https://faculty.georgetown.edu/jod/texts/pliny.html
Summary: Nicomedian church continues as established community in provincial capital. Evidence: Implied from Pliny; general records. Citations: https://faculty.georgetown.edu/jod/texts/pliny.html
Summary: Nicomedian church continues. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Nicomedian church continues; Diocletian makes Nicomedia his Eastern capital (~284). Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250107.htm

Summary: Nicomedia is Diocletian's Eastern capital; Anthimus is bishop; the Great Persecution will begin here in 303 AD. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250108.htm

key_figures: Anthimus of Nicomedia (martyred 303); Eusebius of Nicomedia (young)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Diocletian's Great Persecution begins at Nicomedia (303 AD); the church building is destroyed; Anthimus bishop martyred. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 8.6. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250108.htm

Summary: Eusebius of Nicomedia becomes bishop; he is an Arian sympathizer who will be key to the Arian controversy. Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.4. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Eusebius-of-Nicomedia

Summary: Eusebius of Nicomedia supports Arius at Nicaea (325) and is exiled briefly; Constantine I is baptized by him on his deathbed (337). Evidence: Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.5. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Eusebius-of-Nicomedia

key_figures: Eusebius of Nicomedia; Constantine I (d.337 at Nicomedia)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Constantine I dies at Nicomedia (337 AD) and is baptized by Eusebius of Nicomedia; the Arian party dominates the city. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 4.61. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Constantine-I-Roman-emperor
Summary: Eusebius of Nicomedia dies c.341; Nicomedia remains under Arian influence. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm
Summary: Nicomedia remains an important Eastern city under Arian influence in the post-Nicaea controversies. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Nicaea is a significant Bithynian city; Christianity probable given Pliny's letter attesting widespread Christian presence in the province (~111-112 AD). Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97. Citations: https://faculty.georgetown.edu/jod/texts/pliny.html

Summary: Nicaea church attested through Pliny's province-wide letter; an episcopal community exists in this important Bithynian city. Evidence: Pliny, Ep. 10.96-97. Citations: https://faculty.georgetown.edu/jod/texts/pliny.html

key_figures: Constantine I; Hosius of Corduba; Arius; Alexander of Alexandria; Athanasius
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Nicaea hosts the First Ecumenical Council (325 AD) under Constantine; 318 bishops gather; the Nicene Creed and homoousios formula are adopted against Arianism. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.6-3.14; Theodoret, Ecc. Hist. 1.7. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

Summary: Nicaea church continues in the aftermath of the great council; the city is famous as the site of the 325 council. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm
Summary: Nicaea church continues. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Nicaea is famous as host of the first ecumenical council; the city is now part of the province near Constantinople. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Tradition holds that Andrew the Apostle founded the church at Byzantium and appointed Stachys (Romans 16:9) as first bishop. Uncertainty: tradition first attested in late sources; scholarly consensus places earliest attestation c.100 AD. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 3.4 (via tradition); Romans 16:9. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm
Summary: Tradition continues through Stachys; no contemporary documentation. Uncertainty: tradition retrospective. Evidence: General tradition. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm
Summary: Byzantine church probable as an established see; Byzantium is an important strategic city on the Bosphorus. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250104.htm

Summary: Metrophanes bishop (~306-314); Diocletian siege of Byzantium (306) followed by Constantine's eventual control. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2601.htm
Summary: Metrophanes bishop; Byzantine church is established; the city will soon become Constantine's new capital. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2601.htm

key_figures: Metrophanes of Byzantium; Alexander of Byzantium
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Metrophanes then Alexander of Byzantium bishop; Constantine defeats Licinius at Byzantium (324) and begins transforming it. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.3. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2601.htm

Summary: Constantine refounds Byzantium as Constantinople (dedicated 330 AD); Alexander of Byzantium becomes first bishop of Constantinople; attends Nicaea (325). Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.48. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

key_figures: Alexander of Constantinople; Eusebius of Nicomedia (then)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Constantinople dedicated 330 AD; Alexander bishop; the city is now the imperial capital and the church gains enormous prestige. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 3.48. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Constantine-I-Roman-emperor

key_figures: Paul of Constantinople; Eusebius of Nicomedia; Macedonius
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Constantinople church is deeply contested between Nicene (Paul) and Arian (Eusebius of Nicomedia, Macedonius) factions; Paul expelled multiple times. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Paul of Constantinople martyred (~351) under Constantius II; Macedonius leads the Arian-leaning party; Constantinople becomes an increasingly powerful see. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.2. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Christianity in Roman Britain is probable by the late 2nd/early 3rd century; Tertullian (~197 AD) mentions Britains as reached by Christianity. Uncertainty: Tertullian may be rhetorical exaggeration. Evidence: Tertullian, Adversus Judaeos 7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/0301.htm

Summary: Christianity probable in Roman Britain; Origen (~c.240) also mentions Britain having Christianity. Uncertainty: rhetorical nature of sources. Evidence: Origen, Homilies on Ezekiel 4.1; Tertullian, Adversus Judaeos 7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1208.htm
Summary: Christianity probable in Roman Britain; Londinium is the provincial capital. Evidence: Origen, Homilies (mentions Britain). Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1208.htm
Summary: Christianity probable in Britain; Londinium is center of Roman administration. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm
Summary: Christianity probable in Britain. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Christianity probable in Britain; Alban of Verulamium may have been martyred c.209 or c.304 (dates debated). Uncertainty: dating of Alban's martyrdom is heavily disputed. Evidence: Bede, Ecclesiastical History 1.7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2201.htm
Summary: Christianity probable in Britain; Decian persecution may have affected Britain. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm

Summary: Christianity probable in Britain; Alban of Verulamium martyred c.304 (scholarly date for his martyrdom). Evidence: Bede, Ecc. Hist. 1.7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2201.htm

Summary: Diocletian persecution affects Britain; Alban of Verulamium likely martyred c.304; Aaron and Julius of Caerleon also martyred. Evidence: Bede, Ecc. Hist. 1.7. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2201.htm

key_figures: Restitutus (Bishop of London); Eborius (Bishop of York); Adelfius

Evidence: Council of Arles records (314). Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/Council-of-Arles

Summary: British bishops represented at Nicaea (325) though evidence is indirect; the Church in Britain is an established part of the Western church. Evidence: Nicaea records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

key_figures: Marcellus of Ancyra (bishop from ~314); Vitalis (bishop at Ancyra synod 314)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: The Council of Ancyra (314 AD) addresses apostasy canons; it is a major regional synod that precedes Nicaea. Marcellus of Ancyra becomes an important Nicene theologian. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 10.5; Council of Ancyra canons. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/3801.htm

Summary: Marcellus of Ancyra attends Nicaea (325) and is a strong supporter of the Nicene formula; his own Christology will later be criticized as verging on Marcellitism. Evidence: Nicaea records; Eusebius. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325

Summary: Marcellus of Ancyra is condemned and exiled (~336) by an Eastern synod for his Christology; he appeals to Julius I of Rome. Evidence: Athanasius, Apologia contra Arianos. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2802.htm

Summary: Basil of Ancyra is bishop; he is a leading Semi-Arian (Homoiousian) who opposes both Arius and the Nicene formula. Marcellus is briefly restored then re-exiled. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 3.24. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2604.htm

Summary: Basil of Ancyra leads; Ancyra is a center of Homoiousian theology opposing the strict Arianism of Eudoxius and others. Evidence: Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 4.13. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2604.htm

Summary: Paul and Barnabas preach in the synagogue at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:14-52) on the first missionary journey; Paul's synagogue sermon here is the longest recorded in Acts; they are expelled by local authorities but make many converts. Evidence: Acts 13:14-52. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/13-14.htm

Summary: Paul and Barnabas return to Pisidian Antioch on the way back from their first missionary journey (Acts 14:21-23), strengthening the disciples and appointing elders. Evidence: Acts 14:21-23. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/14-21.htm
Summary: Church at Pisidian Antioch continues with appointed elders. Evidence: Acts 14:23. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/14-23.htm

Summary: Massilia is the major port city of southern Gaul; Christians likely arrive via eastern sea trade routes by the late 2nd century, connected to the broader Gallic church around Lyon. Uncertainty: no direct primary source attests Massilia before the 3rd century. Evidence: Plausible inference from trade routes and Gallic church expansion. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Marseille

Summary: Massilia church probable; the nearby martyrdoms at Lyon and Vienne (177 AD) suggest a broad Gallic Christian network in the region. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.1. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Massilian church is attested by the early 3rd century; the city's role as a major Mediterranean port makes early Christian contact via sea trade highly probable. Evidence: Tertullian references Gallic churches broadly. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Frumentius and his companion Aedesius arrive in Aksum via a shipwreck; they are taken into the royal household and begin to influence the court toward Christianity. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2601016.htm

Summary: Frumentius serves at the Aksumite court; the young king Ezana is educated and influenced toward Christianity. Frumentius later travels to Alexandria to request a bishop from Athanasius. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9; Athanasius, Apol. ad Const. 31. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2601016.htm

Summary: Christianity is gaining favor at the Aksumite court; Ezana's early inscriptions use both traditional pagan and Christian invocations, reflecting a transitional period. Evidence: Aksumite inscriptions. Citations: https://smarthistory.org/aksumite-coins/

Summary: Aksumite coinage shows a shift to overtly Christian symbols after Ezana's conversion (often dated c.340-356), supporting mid-4th-century institutional Christianity. Frumentius is consecrated bishop by Athanasius of Alexandria. Evidence: numismatic/curatorial synthesis; Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9. Citations: https://smarthistory.org/aksumite-coins/ ; https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2601016.htm

Summary: Frumentius (Abba Salama) is the first bishop of Aksum; the Aksumite church adopts a Miaphysite theology aligned with Alexandria; this forms the foundation of the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 10.9; Sozomen, Ecc. Hist. 2.24. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Frumentius

Summary: Paul and Barnabas preach in Lystra (Acts 14:6-20); Paul heals a lame man; crowds try to worship them as Hermes and Zeus; Paul is stoned and left for dead but recovers. Timothy (from Lystra), his mother Eunice and grandmother Lois are believers here. Evidence: Acts 14:6-20; 16:1-3; 2 Tim 1:5. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/14-6.htm

Summary: Paul returns to Lystra on the second missionary journey and recruits Timothy (Acts 16:1-3); the church has established elders. Evidence: Acts 16:1-3. Citations: https://biblehub.com/acts/16-1.htm

Summary: Gregory the Illuminator (born c.257 AD) is a key figure; he will convert the Armenian king and establish the Armenian church. Evidence: Agathangelos, History of Armenia (5th c.). Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Gregory-the-Illuminator

Summary: Gregory the Illuminator is imprisoned by King Tiridates III (~287-298 AD) in the Khor Virap dungeon; he survives 13 years of imprisonment. Evidence: Agathangelos, History of Armenia. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Gregory-the-Illuminator

Summary: Gregory the Illuminator remains imprisoned; small Christian communities exist in Armenia, including some royal court contacts. Evidence: Agathangelos. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Gregory-the-Illuminator

key_figures: Gregory the Illuminator (released); King Tiridates III
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Gregory the Illuminator is released from imprisonment; he heals King Tiridates III who then converts to Christianity (~301 AD). Armenia becomes the first kingdom to adopt Christianity as state religion. Evidence: Agathangelos, History of Armenia. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Gregory-the-Illuminator ; https://www.britannica.com/topic/Armenian-Apostolic-Church

key_figures: Gregory the Illuminator (Catholicos); King Tiridates III
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Gregory the Illuminator is consecrated as bishop / Catholicos by the bishop of Caesarea Cappadocia; the Great Cathedral of Vagharshapat (later Etchmiadzin) is founded. Armenia is the first Christian state. Evidence: Agathangelos. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Armenian-Apostolic-Church

key_figures: Gregory the Illuminator (Catholicos); Aristakes (Gregory's son, at Nicaea)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Aristakes, Gregory the Illuminator's son, represents Armenia at the Council of Nicaea (325); Armenia is firmly in the Nicene camp. Evidence: Nicaea records; Agathangelos. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325 ; https://www.britannica.com/topic/Armenian-Apostolic-Church

Summary: Gregory the Illuminator dies c.328-331; his son Aristakes succeeds as Catholicos. The Armenian church is fully institutionalized. Evidence: Agathangelos. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Gregory-the-Illuminator

Summary: Aristakes is martyred c.333; Vrtanes (Aristakes' brother) becomes Catholicos; the Armenian church faces pressure from both Persia and Arianism. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Armenian-Apostolic-Church

Summary: The Armenian church continues under Vrtanes and Husik I; Armenia is the world's first Christian state and the Armenian Apostolic Church becomes the national church. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Armenian-Apostolic-Church

Summary: Paul explicitly states his intention to visit Spain after Rome (Romans 15:24, 28); if he was released from his first Roman imprisonment (~62 AD) and visited Spain, Tarraco as the provincial capital would be a likely destination. Uncertainty: Paul's Spanish mission is widely debated; Clement of Rome references it (1 Clem 5) but non-specifically. Evidence: Romans 15:24, 28; 1 Clement 5. Citations: https://biblehub.com/romans/15-24.htm ; https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/1010.htm

Summary: Tarraco church probable; the city's role as provincial capital makes early Christian contact plausible. Evidence: General inference from Pauline mission. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2501.htm
Summary: Tarraco church probable. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250103.htm

Summary: Tarraco church probable. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Tarraco church continues after the martyrdom of Fructuosus (259 AD). Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/06317a.htm

Summary: Tarraco church attested; the Council of Elvira (c.306 AD) is the first major Iberian church council and involves bishops from across Spain. Evidence: Council of Elvira records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/05392a.htm

key_figures: Hosius of Corduba (prominent in Spain); unnamed bishops of Tarraco
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Hosius of Corduba is the leading Spanish bishop; Tarraco church attested post-persecution. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250110.htm

key_figures: unnamed bishops; Hosius of Corduba (advisor to Constantine)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Tarraco bishop attends or is affected by Nicaea (325); Hosius of Corduba, Spain's most prominent bishop, presides at Nicaea under Constantine's direction. Evidence: Nicaea records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/event/First-Council-of-Nicaea-325
Summary: Tarraco church attested post-Nicaea. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm
Summary: Tarraco church attested. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/2602.htm

Summary: Tarraco church attested; Fructuosus of Tarragona's 259 AD martyrdom is one of the best-documented 3rd-century martyrdoms in the West. Evidence: Acts of Fructuosus. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/06317a.htm

Summary: Corduba church attested; Hosius of Corduba (born c.256) is becoming active as a young man. He will be bishop of Corduba (~295-357 AD) and one of the most important western theologians. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba
Summary: Corduba church attested; Hosius of Corduba is becoming the leading western bishop. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba

Summary: Hosius of Corduba is bishop (~295-357); he is a leading western theological voice and a close adviser to Constantine I. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba
Summary: Hosius of Corduba leads; he survives the Decian and Valerian persecutions. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba
Summary: Hosius of Corduba continues as bishop. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba

Summary: Hosius of Corduba is bishop; the Council of Elvira (c.306) addresses major disciplinary issues. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/05392a.htm
Summary: Hosius of Corduba is bishop; he plays a key role at the Council of Elvira (c.306). Evidence: Council of Elvira records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/05392a.htm

Summary: Hosius of Corduba presides at the Council of Elvira (~306); this council addresses major disciplinary and moral issues and is one of the earliest complete sets of church canons from the West. Evidence: Council of Elvira records. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/05392a.htm

Summary: Hosius of Corduba becomes Constantine's principal ecclesiastical adviser; he investigates the Donatist controversy in North Africa (c.313) and later presides at Nicaea. Evidence: Eusebius, Life of Constantine 2.63. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba

Summary: Hosius of Corduba presides at Nicaea (325) as Constantine's representative; he may have proposed the Nicene creed formula 'of one substance' (homoousios). He is the most important western churchman of the early 4th century. Evidence: Nicaea records; Athanasius. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba
Summary: Hosius of Corduba continues as bishop; he attends the Council of Sardica (343-344). Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba
Summary: Hosius of Corduba continues; he resists Constantius II's Arian pressure until his final years. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba

Summary: Hosius of Corduba (d.357) is the dominant western church leader of the 4th century; his presiding at Nicaea and close relationship with Constantine shaped the Nicene settlement. Evidence: Athanasius, Hist. Ar. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Hosius-of-Cordoba

Summary: Dura-Europos is a Roman frontier city on the Euphrates; Christian presence not yet attested in this decade. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Dura-Europos
Summary: Dura-Europos church not yet attested in this decade. Evidence: General records. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Dura-Europos

Summary: Dura-Europos church probable by this decade; architectural renovation of a house into a Christian meeting place likely underway. Evidence: Archaeological evidence (Dura-Europos excavations). Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Dura-Europos

key_figures: unnamed bishops; unknown house church leader
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: The Dura-Europos house church (domus ecclesiae) is the earliest archaeologically known Christian house church, dated c.232-240 AD by inscription and stratigraphy; it contains baptistery paintings of Christ healing the paralytic, the women at the tomb, and David and Goliath. Evidence: Kraeling, The Excavations at Dura Europos (1967). Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Dura-Europos ; https://smarthistory.org/dura-europos/

Summary: Dura-Europos house church active; the building has a dedicated baptistery with Christian wall paintings—among the earliest known Christian art. Evidence: Kraeling, Dura Europos excavations. Citations: https://smarthistory.org/dura-europos/
Summary: Dura-Europos house church continues; the city is under threat from Sasanid Persia. Evidence: Archaeological evidence. Citations: https://smarthistory.org/dura-europos/

Summary: Dura-Europos is captured and destroyed by the Sasanid Persians under Shapur I c.256-257 AD; the Christian house church is buried under Persian siege embankments, which ironically preserved it until modern excavation. Evidence: Archaeological evidence. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/place/Dura-Europos

Summary: Samosata is the former capital of Commagene; Christian presence probable via Syrian missions by the mid-1st century. Uncertainty: no direct 1st-century source attests Samosata. Summary: Georgian tradition claims Andrew the Apostle preached in Iberia; this tradition is late and unattested before the medieval period. Uncertainty: apostolic founding tradition is not historically corroborated. Evidence: Later Georgian tradition only. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Georgian-Orthodox-Apostolic-Church

Summary: Christian presence probable in Iberia by the late 3rd century via Armenian influence (Armenia converts c.301 AD) and trade routes from Cappadocia and Pontus. Evidence: General inference; proximity to Armenia. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Georgian-Orthodox-Apostolic-Church

key_figures: Nino of Cappadocia (young slave girl); unnamed missionaries
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Nino of Cappadocia (c.296-338 AD) arrives in Iberia as a slave girl or refugee and begins preaching Christianity. Evidence: Life of Nino (Kartlis Tskhovreba). Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Nino

Summary: Nino of Cappadocia continues missionary activity in Iberia; her healing of Queen Nana gains royal attention. Evidence: Life of Nino. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Nino

key_figures: Nino of Cappadocia; King Mirian III
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: Nino of Cappadocia heals King Mirian III who was lost in a forest; he converts to Christianity c.326-337 AD (precise date debated). Iberia becomes officially Christian, making it among the first states to do so after Armenia. Evidence: Life of Nino; Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 1.10. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Nino ; https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/27011.htm

key_figures: Nino of Cappadocia; King Mirian III; first bishop (sent from Constantinople)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: King Mirian III requests bishops and priests from Constantine I; the first bishop of Iberia is ordained; the Svetitskhoveli Cathedral site (Mtskheta) is established. Iberia is officially Christian c.327-337 AD. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 1.10; Life of Nino. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/27011.htm

key_figures: first bishop of Iberia; Nino of Cappadocia (d.c.338)
evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: The Iberian church is fully established; Nino of Cappadocia dies c.338 AD and is venerated as Equal-to-the-Apostles; Mtskheta becomes the see of the Catholicos of all Georgia. Evidence: Life of Nino. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/biography/Saint-Nino

Summary: Mtskheta is the ecclesiastical capital of Iberia (Georgia); Nino's conversion of King Mirian III makes Georgia (along with Armenia) one of the first officially Christian states in history. Evidence: Rufinus, Ecc. Hist. 1.10; Life of Nino. Citations: https://www.britannica.com/topic/Georgian-Orthodox-Apostolic-Church

Summary: Pepuza is declared the 'New Jerusalem' by Montanus (c.156-172 AD), a charismatic Phrygian prophet; together with Priscilla and Maximilla (female prophets), he founds the Montanist ('New Prophecy') movement here. The movement emphasizes direct prophetic inspiration, strict asceticism, and an imminent Second Coming. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16-19; Epiphanius, Panarion 48-49. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm ; https://www.britannica.com/topic/Montanism

Summary: Montanism spreads rapidly from Pepuza across Asia Minor; the proto-orthodox leadership in Asia holds synods to condemn the New Prophecy; Apollinaris of Hierapolis writes against it. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16; Apollinaris of Hierapolis. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Montanus, Priscilla, and Maximilla are dead by this period; Pepuza remains the Montanist center and pilgrimage destination. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.18. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Pepuza remains the Montanist headquarters; Tertullian in Carthage becomes a Montanist sympathizer c.206 AD; the movement spreads to North Africa, Rome, and Gaul. Evidence: Tertullian, De Ecstasi; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.18. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Pepuza continues as Montanist center; proto-orthodox church holds councils in Asia condemning Montanism. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.16-19. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250105.htm

Summary: Pepuza continues as Montanist pilgrimage center; Tertullian writes pro-Montanist tracts from Carthage (~207-220 AD). Evidence: Tertullian, De Monogamia; Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 5.18. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250106.htm

Summary: Bethany is the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus — Jesus's closest friends outside the Twelve. Jesus raises Lazarus from the dead here (John 11:1-44), his most dramatic miracle before the crucifixion; he is also anointed here (Matt 26:6-13; John 12:1-8); and the ascension tradition is associated with the road from Bethany to the Mount of Olives (Luke 24:50-51). Evidence: John 11:1-44; Luke 24:50-51. Citations: https://biblehub.com/john/11-1.htm

evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: The Abgar Legend holds that King Abgar V of Edessa wrote to Jesus requesting healing and received a reply letter; after Jesus's death, the apostle Thaddaeus (Addai) is said to have come to heal and evangelize the king. Eusebius claims to have found the original Aramaic correspondence in the Edessene royal archives (Ecc. Hist. 1.13). The historical core is uncertain but Edessa has one of the oldest attested Christian communities east of the Euphrates. Evidence: Eusebius, Ecc. Hist. 1.13; Doctrina Addai. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/fathers/250101.htm

Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm

Summary: Thebes church attested; Pachomius converts to Christianity c.313 AD after experiencing Christian charity during his conscripted military service. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm

key_figures: unnamed bishops; Pachomius (founds Tabennisi monastery c.318-320 AD)

Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius; Athanasius. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm

Summary: Thebes church attested; Pachomius's Tabennisi monastery grows rapidly; the Pachomian monastic federation eventually includes 9 monasteries and 2 nunneries. Evidence: Nicaea records; Pachomian chronicles. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm
Summary: Thebes church attested; Pachomian monastic federation grows across the Thebaid. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm

Summary: Thebes church attested; Pachomius dies of plague in 348 AD; his monastic Rule influences Benedict of Nursia and all Western monasticism through John Cassian. Evidence: Coptic Lives of Pachomius. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm

Summary: Thebes church attested; the Thebaid region — with Anthony the Great's eremitic tradition and Pachomius's cenobitic monastic federation — is the birthplace of Christian monasticism, which becomes the most influential form of organized Christian life outside parish churches. Evidence: Athanasius, Life of Anthony; Coptic Lives of Pachomius. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/11387b.htm

Summary: Tiberias is the capital built by Herod Antipas c.20 AD on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee, named for Emperor Tiberius; the lake is called 'Sea of Tiberias' in John 6:1 and 21:1; Jesus reportedly avoided the city (built over a cemetery, unclean to Jews), but his ministry centers on its surrounding shores. Christian presence probable from the Galilean mission. Evidence: John 6:1; John 21:1; general records. Citations: https://biblehub.com/john/6-1.htm

evidence_notes_and_citations: Summary: The Council of Elvira (~306 AD) is held here — the first major church council in the Western Roman Empire; 19 bishops and 26 priests from all parts of Spain attend; it issues 81 canons covering clerical celibacy, mixed marriages, lapsi, and excommunication — producing the earliest extant comprehensive Western canon law. Evidence: Council of Elvira canons. Citations: https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/05389a.htm
